nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving to still freshwater rivers and canals over sandy and muddy substrates; quiet zones near cascades; benthopelagic bottom-dweller in the Saramacca, Suriname, and Marowijne River drainages
nativeRange.biotope: Guiana coastal river — sandy-bottom slow water

decorPreferences: deep fine sand substrate (mandatory — minimum 5 cm depth for sifting behaviour)
large smooth river stones and driftwood as territory markers and visual breaks
overhanging roots and submerged bogwood for shelter
robust potted background plants (Amazon swords, Vallisneria) — avoid delicate foreground plants
open sand foraging areas in the foreground with sheltered zones behind decor
dim to moderate lighting; shade areas with floating plants

diet.notes: Obligate sand-sifting forager: takes mouthfuls of substrate and sieves invertebrates, insect larvae, small crustaceans, worms, and organic detritus through specialised gill rakers, expelling sand from the operculum. In aquaria accepts high-quality sinking pellets, frozen/live bloodworms, daphnia, brine shrimp, white worms, mysis, and occasional blanched vegetables or spirulina wafers. Gravel or coarse substrate MUST NOT be used — particles damage the delicate gill rakers during sifting, causing chronic injury. Feed 2–3 times daily with a varied diet.

goodWith: other large peaceful Guiana/Amazonian cichlids (Satanoperca, Geophagus spp., Acarichthys)
large South American tetras (Silver Dollars, Bleeding Heart tetras, larger Hyphessobrycon spp.)
large peaceful catfish (Ancistrus, Hypancistrus, Corydoras of comparable origin)
peaceful open-water cichlids of similar size
avoidWith: small nano fish and dwarf shrimp (predation or harassment risk during sand-sifting)
highly aggressive cichlids (Oscars, Midas, Red Devils) — will be outcompeted
bottom-dwelling territorial species competing for substrate zone
delicate substrate-rooting plants (uprooted by constant sifting)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Geophagus surinamensis; verify URL availability before download.
Wikimedia Commons candidate; verify current URL and license attribution on the file page before use.

summary: The Surinamensis Eartheater is a mid-large Guiana cichlid (20–25 cm) that earns its name by continuously sifting mouthfuls of sand for invertebrates. Native to the Suriname, Saramacca, and Marowijne River systems. Requires a 400 L+ tank with fine sand substrate at least 5 cm deep — gravel will destroy its delicate gill rakers. A larvophilous mouthbrooder with biparental care.
careNotes:
Geophagus surinamensis is a mid-to-large South American cichlid from the Guiana Shield river systems of Suriname and French Guiana, distinct from the more widely available Amazonian congeners such as G. altifrons. In the hobby, fish sold as ‘surinamensis’ are often misidentified G. altifrons or related species — confirmed G. surinamensis tends to be rarer in the trade.

The single most critical husbandry requirement is substrate: fine sand is mandatory. G. surinamensis feeds by scooping mouthfuls of sand, filtering invertebrates and organic matter through specialised gill rakers, and expelling the substrate from the operculum. Gravel or coarse material physically lacerates these delicate structures, leading to chronic injury, secondary bacterial infection, and eventual death. A sand bed of at minimum 5 cm depth is required; deeper beds (8–10 cm) are preferable to allow uninhibited sifting.

A 400 L tank with at least a 150 cm footprint is the practical minimum for a group of 5–6 individuals. Keep in soft to moderately hard, neutral-to-slightly-acidic water (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 5–15, 24–28°C) mirroring Guiana coastal river conditions. Regular large water changes (30–50% weekly) are essential; this species is highly sensitive to deteriorating water quality and should never be introduced to a biologically immature aquarium. Over-filtration with canister filters and/or a sump system is strongly recommended; diffuse the output to maintain slow current.

Despite being a cichlid, G. surinamensis is generally one of the more peaceful species in the family toward dissimilar tankmates, though spawning pairs may become temporarily territorial around their substrate pit. Keep in groups of 5+ to distribute any intraspecific social pressure and allow natural pair formation.
breedingNotes:
Geophagus surinamensis is a larvophilous mouthbrooder that practises biparental care. Eggs are deposited on a flat surface (smooth stone, root, or a cleared patch of sand) and fertilised. Unlike ovophilous mouthbrooders, the parents do not immediately pick up the eggs; instead, both adults guard the clutch on the substrate until hatching, at which point the wriggling larvae are taken into the buccal cavity of one or both parents. Larvae are incubated and protected in the mouth for several weeks until the fry are free-swimming and capable of limited independent foraging.

Conditioning the group with varied live and frozen foods (bloodworms, brine shrimp, white worms) for several weeks before spawning is strongly recommended. A minimum group of 5 individuals increases the probability of natural pair formation. In a community aquarium the chances of fry survival are low; isolating the pair or separating from the group after a few spawns improves outcomes. First foods for free-swimming fry include baby brine shrimp, micro-worms, and finely powdered fry food. Parental care is extensive and fry are recalled into the parents’ mouths when threatened.

primary.name: FishBase — Geophagus surinamensis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided max size (17.2 cm SL; up to 30 cm TL cited), temperature range (22–25°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (5–19 dH), native range (Saramacca and Suriname Rivers in Suriname; Marowijne River in Suriname and French Guiana), habitat (mud and sand-bottomed canals, quiet zones near cascades), diet classification (omnivore with herbivorous tendency, substrate-sifting via protractile mouth), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes, Geophaginae), and commercial aquarium use confirmation. Temperature range conservatively raised to 24–28°C based on consensus across additional sources; FishBase lower bound of 22°C is the species’ minimum tolerance, not the recommended keeping range.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory — Geophagus surinamensis Care Guide
additional.notes: Supplemented adult size (25–30 cm TL in optimal conditions, 2–3 years to full maturity), temperature (24–27°C), pH (6.0–8.0), tank guidance, breeding behaviour (larvophilous mouthbrooder, flat-surface spawning, oral incubation), compatibility (peaceful toward most species; territorial only during spawning), lifespan (5–10 years), and sand substrate requirement (gravel damages gills).

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Suriname Eartheater (Geophagus proximus)
additional.notes: Page covers G. proximus (a closely related congener often sold interchangeably with G. surinamensis in the trade). Cross-checked size range (18–30 cm), temperature (24–28°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (2–12°N), minimum tank volume (250–500 L depending on group size), native range (Guyana, Suriname, northern Brazil drainages), diet (sinking pellets, frozen/live, vegetable matter), breeding (biparental mouthbrooder, flat-surface spawn), lifespan (8–10 years), and mandatory fine-sand substrate requirement.

additional.name: LiveAquaria — Redstriped Eartheater Cichlid
additional.notes: Trade retail source. Provided max size (~26 cm), temperature (22–25°C), pH (6.5–7.5), hardness (dKH 9–11), minimum tank size (100 gal / ~380 L), diet (freeze-dried bloodworms, tubifex, ocean plankton, flake food, cichlid pellets), breeding mode (larvophile mouthbrooder, biparental), substrate requirement (sandy bottom with stone/root caves and hardy potted plants), and compatibility note (suitable with large catfish; territorial during spawning).

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Picking the Right Eartheater for Your Tank
additional.notes: Editorial hobbyist reference. Confirmed G. surinamensis exceeds 20 cm, suited to 400–500 L minimum for a group of six adults, and is one of the larger Geophagus species appropriate for experienced beginners with spacious aquaria.

fish.breedingNotes: Larvophilous mouthbrooder with biparental care: eggs guarded on flat substrate surface; wriggling larvae (not eggs) taken into buccal cavity at hatching. Oral incubation continues for several weeks until fry are free-swimming. Brood conditioning with live/frozen foods recommended. Minimum group of 5 to allow natural pair formation. Community fry survival low without pair isolation.
